title: Leaf-drape bowl in paper clay
subtitle: A bowl shaped over a real large leaf as a natural mould
excerpt: A leaf-drape bowl uses a large leaf, rhubarb, hosta, or fatsia, as a natural hump mould. A paper clay slab is draped over the leaf, which supports the clay while it dries and transfers the leaf's veining as a textured impression on the inside of the bowl. The leaf is left underneath and falls away as the clay dries. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Leaf-drape mould technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A large leaf serves as a ready-made

slab while the clay stiffens, then dries out and falls away cleanly once the bowl is leather-hard. The veining of the leaf transfers as a raised pattern on the inside of the bowl. Rhubarb, hosta, fatsia, and large fig leaves all work well; the leaf needs to be large enough to support a bowl 20 to 25 cm across.

attrs.body: Drape the clay onto the underside of the leaf, the side with the raised veins. This presses the veins into the clay surface, creating a raised impression in the finished bowl. The leaf's top (smooth) side gives very little texture.
attrs.title: Leaf side matters

items.name: Large fresh leaf (rhubarb, hosta, or fatsia), 25 to 35 cm across
Pottery rolling pin and 6 mm guide rails

content.text: Place the leaf underside-up on a flat surface. The leaf will curl slightly at the edges: this is fine and adds to the bowl's natural curve. Do not add any release agent, the leaf dries and separates cleanly on its own.
Step 2: Roll and drape the slab

Roll a slab 6 mm thick and slightly larger than the leaf. Fold in half, carry to the leaf, and unfold over the leaf surface from the centre outward. Press down gently with a damp sponge to make full contact with the leaf veins, press the sponge rather than the hand so you do not push through the clay.
Trim around the edge of the leaf or to a smaller oval if preferred, using the fettling knife. Run a damp finger along the cut rim to smooth the edge.
Leave the clay on the leaf undisturbed for four to six hours until it reaches

. The clay will pull slightly away from the leaf as it stiffens. Turn the bowl right-side-up: the leaf usually falls away on its own, or can be peeled back gently. Any leaf fragments that stick can be brushed off with a dry brush once the clay is bone-dry. Seal with PVA diluted 1:1.
Make a set of three bowls in different sizes. Use a fatsia leaf for the largest, a hosta for the medium, and a geranium or ivy leaf for the smallest dish.
Paint the bowl interior with acrylic before sealing: the raised veins catch a dry-brushed highlight well.

attrs.intro: Problems when making the leaf-drape bowl.

items.fix: Ensure the leaf is underside-up. Press the sponge firmly and evenly over the whole surface. The veins should be clearly visible in the wet clay before leaving to dry.
items.cause: Sponge pressure was too light or the leaf was placed smooth-side-up.
items.symptom: Leaf detail barely shows on the finished bowl

items.fix: Press the knife straight down through the clay in a single movement. Run a damp finger along the cut rim immediately to smooth and compress the edge.
items.cause: Fettling knife dragged rather than cutting straight down.
items.symptom: Rim cracks where the slab was trimmed

attrs.heading: Common problems
